cards.def: To attack someone or something vigorously; to bombard with criticism or questions.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "The candidate was assailed with questions about the scandal at every turn."
cards.context: ETS uses 'assail' for relentless, multi-directional attack — verbal, critical, or physical. The intensity is sustained, not a single blow. Often appears in passages about public figures facing scrutiny.
cards.mnem: ASSAIL → AS + SAIL → sails catching wind from all sides — attacked from every direction like a ship in a storm.
cards.vis: A ship labeled 'CANDIDATE' is caught in a storm. Wind attacks from the left. Rain from the right. Waves from below. Every SAIL is being hit simultaneously. The crew can't steer — they're being AS-SAIL-ed from all directions at once. No angle of safety. ASSAIL = every SAIL under attack.

cards.def: Well-meaning and kindly; generous and charitable in intention toward others.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "The benevolent founder donated half the company's profits to education annually."
cards.context: ETS uses 'benevolent' for kindness that is actively expressed — not just feeling kind but doing kind things. Distinguish from 'magnanimous' (generous after victory) and 'munificent' (lavishly generous).
cards.mnem: BENE + VOLENT → BENE (good, Latin) + VOLENT (willing) → WILLING to do GOOD — goodwill in action.
cards.vis: A doctor's coat has 'BENE' stitched on one side and 'VOLENT' on the other. She walks into the poorest neighborhood, bag open, WILLING to give everything inside. The BENE and VOLENT labels glow as she works. She's not just good — she's WILLING to be good. BENE-VOLENT.

cards.def: To scold or criticize someone angrily and at length.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "The manager berated the employee for the error in front of the entire team."
cards.context: ETS uses 'berate' for sustained, angry verbal punishment — longer and more personal than 'chastise,' less formal than 'censure.' The anger is front and center.
cards.mnem: BERATE → BE + RATE → to RATE someone's behavior and give them a BE(ating) with your words — a zero-star review delivered furiously.
cards.vis: A manager holds up a review card for an employee: '0 STARS.' Then she starts RATING every single failure — loudly, angrily, one by one. The employee shrinks. Each RATE-ing is delivered as a verbal BE-ating. BE-RATE: the zero-star review that never ends.

cards.def: Able to stay afloat; cheerful and optimistic; not easily depressed.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "Despite repeated setbacks, she maintained a buoyant optimism that inspired her team."
cards.context: ETS uses 'buoyant' figuratively for emotional resilience — someone whose spirits keep floating back up no matter what pushes them down. Also used in economics: a buoyant market rises.
cards.mnem: BUOYANT → BUOY + ANT → a BUOY that floats no matter what — even with an ANT sitting on it, it bobs back up.
cards.vis: A tiny ANT sits on a BUOY in stormy seas. Waves crash over it. The BUOY goes under — then pops back up. The ANT waves cheerfully. Another wave: under, then up again. The ANT never stops smiling. BUOY + ANT: unsinkable optimism, floating back up every single time.

cards.def: To support or strengthen a structure, argument, or position; a prop or source of support.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "The new evidence buttressed the prosecution's case considerably."
cards.context: ETS uses 'buttress' figuratively for reinforcing arguments or positions — adding structural support so they don't collapse under pressure. The architecture metaphor is always active.
cards.mnem: BUTTRESS → BUTT + PRESS → you PRESS your BUTT against a wall to stop it from falling — your body is the buttress.
cards.vis: A medieval cathedral wall leans dangerously. A knight plants his BUTT against the stone wall and PRESSES with all his might to hold it up. His body is the buttress — structural support through sheer force. BUTT + PRESS = BUTTRESS. Holding the argument upright with your body weight.

cards.def: To accept or allow behavior considered wrong to continue; to overlook or forgive an offense.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "The administration was accused of condoning corruption by failing to investigate it."
cards.context: ETS uses 'condone' when inaction implies approval — not actively endorsing, but tacitly allowing. It carries implicit criticism of passive acceptance of wrongdoing.
cards.mnem: CONDONE → CON + DONE → a CON that's been DONE — and you're letting it slide, acting like it's done and finished with.
cards.vis: A CON artist finishes a scam in plain view. A supervisor watches, shrugs, and stamps the file 'DONE.' No punishment. No investigation. The CON is DONE — and allowed to stay done. CON-DONE: the supervisor's stamp is the act of condoning. Passive acceptance of the con.

cards.def: To violate or go against a rule, law, or principle; to conflict with.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "The new regulation contravened established environmental protections."
cards.context: ETS uses 'contravene' specifically for violation of established rules or laws — not just disagreement but active transgression of a defined standard.
cards.mnem: CONTRA + VENE → CONTRA (against) + VENE (come/go) → to GO AGAINST — walk directly into the rule, head-on.
cards.vis: A law stands as a solid wall in a corridor labeled 'ESTABLISHED RULE.' A regulation walks in and — instead of going around — walks STRAIGHT THROUGH the wall, head-on. CONTRA: against. VENE: to go. CONTRAVENE: to go directly against, without detour, without apology. The wall crumbles.

cards.def: To publicly declare as wicked or wrong; to formally accuse or condemn.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "World leaders denounced the military coup as an attack on democracy."
cards.context: ETS uses 'denounce' for formal, public, named condemnation — more specific than 'decry' (general disapproval) because it targets a particular person or act publicly.
cards.mnem: DE + NOUNCE → DE-ANNOUNCE → to ANNOUNCE against something — make a public declaration aimed downward at a target.
cards.vis: A herald climbs a tower and makes an ANNOUNCEMENT — but instead of praising, it's aimed DOWN at a specific target below: 'I hereby DE-ANNOUNCE you — you are WRONG, WICKED, and NAMED.' The target looks up, exposed. DE-NOUNCE: the announcement that strips rather than crowns. Pointed. Public. Damning.

cards.def: Exercising power in a cruel and absolute way; tyrannical.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "The despotic ruler silenced all opposition and controlled the press completely."
cards.context: ETS uses 'despotic' to describe governance by unchecked, absolute power exercised without regard for others' rights — the structural concentration of all authority in one person.
cards.mnem: DESPOTIC → DESPOT + IC → a DESPOT rules with an iron fist — full of DESPOT-IC energy: absolute, crushing, unchallenged.
cards.vis: A DESPOT sits on a throne so tall it scrapes the clouds. He has one enormous iron fist and one enormous iron boot — the fist crushes dissent, the boot stamps on rights. Both are labeled 'DESPOTIC AUTHORITY.' No one stands near the throne. The distance is the power. DESPOT + IC = total control.

cards.def: To depart from an established course, standard, or expectation; to diverge.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "The pilot deviated from the flight path to avoid the storm."
cards.context: ETS uses 'deviate' when someone or something departs from the norm, rule, or plan — may be neutral (practical deviation) or negative (violation of standards). Context determines tone.
cards.mnem: DEVIATE → DE + VIA + TE → to leave the VIA (path) — to step OFF the designated road entirely.
cards.vis: A straight VIA (Roman road) stretches ahead. A traveler walks confidently — then veers DE off the path into a field. A sign behind them reads 'VIA' with an arrow pointing to the abandoned road. DE-VIA-TE: left the VIA. The road goes one way; she goes another. The deviation is visible from space.

cards.def: Not influenced by personal gain; impartial and unbiased.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "We need a disinterested party to mediate the dispute fairly."
cards.context: ETS's most notorious trap: 'disinterested' ≠ 'uninterested' (bored). Disinterested = having no personal stake, therefore impartial. A disinterested judge is IDEAL; an uninterested judge is a problem.
cards.mnem: DIS + INTERESTED → no personal INTEREST in the outcome — you have nothing to gain, so your judgment stays pure.
cards.vis: Two parties fight over a pie. The judge is brought in. She looks at the pie. She doesn't want any. She has no INTEREST in the pie. Her ruling is therefore CLEAN — no bias, no hunger, no stake. DIS-INTERESTED: zero personal interest = perfect impartiality. The pie means nothing to her. That's the point.

cards.def: To increase rapidly in intensity or severity; to cause something to become more serious.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "The minor disagreement escalated into a full diplomatic crisis within hours."
cards.context: ETS uses 'escalate' for situations that intensify beyond their starting point — often used when something small grows dangerously large through successive steps.
cards.mnem: ESCALATE → ESCALATOR → stepping onto an ESCALATOR that only goes UP — no stopping, no reversing, level by level.
cards.vis: A small argument steps onto an ESCALATOR. The ESCALATOR only goes up. First floor: disagreement. Second: raised voices. Third: threats. Fourth: international incident. The argument rides the ESCALATOR helplessly upward, each level more serious than the last. ESCAL-ATE: the escalator ate everything on the way up.

cards.def: To drive out an evil spirit; to rid oneself of something troubling or deeply negative.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "Writing the memoir helped her exorcise the guilt she had carried for decades."
cards.context: ETS uses 'exorcise' figuratively — driving out psychological demons, past traumas, or deeply embedded negative patterns. Distinguish from 'exercise' (physical activity).
cards.mnem: EXORCISE → EX + ORC + ISE → drive out the ORC (monster) — EX-ORC-ISE: throw the monster OUT of you.
cards.vis: A tiny ORC sits inside a person's chest, rattling around, causing misery. An exorcist opens the chest door, grabs the ORC by its collar, and throws it EX (out). The door slams. The ORC tumbles away into the distance. EX-ORC-ISE: the ORC is removed. The chest is clean. The person breathes freely for the first time.

cards.def: Excessively particular about details; difficult to please; fussy.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "The finicky editor rejected twelve drafts before approving the final version."
cards.context: ETS uses 'finicky' for unreasonable particularity — attention to detail beyond what is productive. Similar to 'fastidious' but with a more negative, petulant flavor.
cards.mnem: FINICKY → FIN + ICK + Y → FINds ICKiness everywhere — everything triggers an 'ICK' response.
cards.vis: A food critic sits before a perfect meal. She picks up the fork — ICK, wrong angle. The plate — ICK, wrong temperature. The garnish — ICK, too green. Her FIN (nose) crinkles at each ICK. FIN-ICK-Y: the FIN (nose) finds ICK in everything. Nothing escapes the ICK detector.

cards.def: To prevent something from succeeding; a person or thing that contrasts with another to highlight their qualities.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "The detective foiled the robbery just in time." / "Watson serves as a foil to Holmes's brilliance."
cards.context: ETS tests both senses: the verb (thwart/prevent) and the literary noun (a character who contrasts with another to emphasize qualities). Context — narrative vs. action — determines which.
cards.mnem: FOIL → ALUMINUM FOIL → foil WRAPS and STOPS things from getting through — or it REFLECTS what's next to it, making contrasts visible.
cards.vis: A villain's plan is wrapped in ALUMINUM FOIL by the hero — completely sealed, stopped, thwarted. Then the foil is held next to a shiny character: their brilliance REFLECTS off the dull foil beside them. FOIL: wraps and stops the plan (verb) — or reflects contrast by its presence (noun). Same material. Two jobs.

cards.def: Twisted or woven together; closely connected or linked so as to be inseparable.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "The economic and political crises were so intertwined that solving one required addressing both."
cards.context: ETS uses 'intertwined' for relationships or issues that are structurally bound — pulling one thread moves the other. Appears in passages about complex, mutually dependent phenomena.
cards.mnem: INTER + TWINED → BETWEEN + TWISTED → two threads twisted between each other — you cannot separate them without unraveling both.
cards.vis: Two ropes labeled 'ECONOMY' and 'POLITICS' are being twisted together on a loom. A policy analyst tries to pull just one strand out. The other follows. She pulls harder. Both unravel. They are INTER-TWINED: twisted BETWEEN each other so deeply that separation destroys both. One move. Two consequences.

cards.def: To overwhelm with a large quantity of things; to flood.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "The office was inundated with complaint calls after the product recall."
cards.context: ETS uses 'inundate' for volume that overwhelms capacity — a flood of work, requests, or information that exceeds what can be handled. Always implies excess beyond normal.
cards.mnem: INUNDATE → IN + UNDA (wave, Latin) → wave after wave pouring IN — you're underwater before you can respond.
cards.vis: An office worker sits calmly at her desk. Then — one UNDA (wave) of emails. Then another. And another. They pour IN through the walls — literal blue waves crashing against her monitor, filling the room. She's submerged up to her chin, still trying to type. IN-UNDA-TE: wave after wave IN. She can't swim out.

cards.def: Impossible to contradict, break, or change; completely firm and binding.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "The lawyers drew up an ironclad contract with no loopholes."
cards.context: ETS uses 'ironclad' for arguments, agreements, or rules that cannot be dismantled — the metaphor is a warship clad in iron, impervious to attack. Maximum strength/certainty.
cards.mnem: IRON + CLAD → a warship CLAD IN IRON — nothing penetrates it. The armor IS the argument.
cards.vis: A contract glows on a table. A lawyer tries to find a loophole — pokes it with a pen. The pen bounces off. She tries scissors — they snap. A battering ram — it CLANGS and falls backward. The contract is literally CLAD IN IRON. Every attack fails. IRON-CLAD: armored from every challenge, impervious to breach.

cards.def: To put someone or something into a situation of risk or danger.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "Leaking the information would jeopardize the entire undercover operation."
cards.context: ETS uses 'jeopardize' when an action introduces risk to something previously secure — it was safe, now it isn't. The recklessness is implicit: someone caused the danger.
cards.mnem: JEOPARDIZE → JEOPARDY + IZE → to make something enter JEOPARDY — to place it on the Jeopardy stage where everything is at risk for a single wrong answer.
cards.vis: A perfectly secure operation is placed on the JEOPARDY game show stage — buzzer in hand, under bright lights, one wrong answer away from total loss. Everything: the mission, the agents, the years of work — now a JEOPARDY answer. JEOPARD-IZE: to drag the safe thing onto that stage.

cards.def: Subject to sudden or unpredictable changes of mood or behavior; volatile.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "His mercurial temperament made it impossible to predict how he'd respond to feedback."
cards.context: ETS uses 'mercurial' for personalities or situations that shift unpredictably — named after Mercury, the fastest planet and Roman god of speed and change.
cards.mnem: MERCURIAL → MERCURY → liquid MERCURY: impossible to hold — it splits, rolls, changes shape constantly without warning.
cards.vis: Someone tries to hold MERCURY in their palm. It splits into a dozen droplets instantly. They chase one — it rolls away. They cup their hands — it flows through the cracks. Every attempt to contain it fails. This is a MERCURIAL temperament: the moment you think you understand it, it has become something else entirely.

cards.def: Not aware of or not concerned about what is happening around one; unmindful.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "He was completely oblivious to the chaos his decision had caused."
cards.context: ETS uses 'oblivious' when a character lacks awareness of crucial surrounding information — often paired with dramatic irony where the reader sees what the character cannot.
cards.mnem: OBLIVIOUS → OBLIVION + OUS → full of OBLIVION — your awareness has fallen into the void, erased.
cards.vis: A building is on fire. The fire alarm screams. Everyone evacuates. One person sits at their desk, headphones on, peacefully typing. Their awareness has fallen into OBLIVION — a black hole behind their eyes where the burning world should be registering. OB-LIVION-OUS: existence deleted from perception.

cards.def: To carry out or commit a harmful, illegal, or immoral act.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "The fraud was perpetrated over several years using falsified documents."
cards.context: ETS uses 'perpetrate' exclusively for harmful, criminal, or wrongful acts — you perpetrate a crime, a fraud, a deception. Never used neutrally. The perpetrator is always doing something wrong.
cards.mnem: PERPETRATE → PERP + ETRATE → the PERP (perpetrator) ETRATES (penetrates) into the crime — executes it from the inside.
cards.vis: A PERP in a trench coat slides through a 'CRIME ZONE' door labeled 'FRAUD INSIDE.' He doesn't just plan it — he ETRATES: penetrates the crime, executes it from within. He IS the perpetration. PERP + ETRATE: the perp who gets inside the act and does it. Active, deliberate, personal.

cards.def: Sounding sad and mournful; expressing sorrow.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "The plaintive cry of the violin echoed through the empty concert hall."
cards.context: ETS uses 'plaintive' for sounds, tones, or expressions that carry inherent sadness — music, voices, or animal sounds that communicate mourning or longing without words.
cards.mnem: PLAINTIVE → PLAIN + TIVE → a PLAIN, flat landscape of sadness — mournful, stretching, with nowhere to hide from the grief.
cards.vis: A violin plays alone in a PLAIN — flat, grey, endless. The note stretches to the horizon. There's no echo, no warmth, no shelter. Just sound traveling across emptiness. The PLAIN amplifies the sorrow. PLAIN-TIVE: grief played across an open plain, so exposed it hurts to hear. Sadness with nowhere to go.

cards.def: Evoking a keen sense of sadness or regret; deeply affecting emotionally.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "The poignant letter from the soldier was read aloud at the memorial service."
cards.context: ETS uses 'poignant' for emotional sharpness — not just sadness but piercing, precisely targeted sadness that reaches something specific inside the reader. From French 'poindre': to sting.
cards.mnem: POIGNANT → POINT + ANT → a tiny ANT with a POINTED sting that hits your emotions with surgical precision — small but sharp.
cards.vis: A tiny ANT in army boots walks up to a person's heart and delivers one perfectly POINTED sting — not a punch, not a flood, just a single precise pierce. The person's eyes fill immediately. The ANT is POIGN-ANT: the POINTED ANT that finds exactly the right emotional spot and stings it. Small. Exact. Devastating.

cards.def: In a state of quiet inactivity; dormant or temporarily at rest.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "The volcano had been quiescent for two centuries before suddenly erupting."
cards.context: ETS uses 'quiescent' for things that appear calm but are not necessarily resolved — a temporary stillness that may conceal ongoing potential. Often used in geology, politics, or psychology.
cards.mnem: QUIESCENT → QUIET + ESCENT → becoming QUIET — the -ESCENT suffix means 'in the process of becoming.' It is in the process of being quiet.
cards.vis: A volcano sits perfectly still — no smoke, no tremor. A 'QUIESCENT' sign hangs on it. But deep underground, the magma is still there, still hot, still waiting. The QUIET is active: it is being QUIET right now, as a state in progress. QUIET + ESCENT: not dead. Just being very, very quiet. For now.

cards.def: To say or do something again or repeatedly, for emphasis or clarity.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "The professor reiterated the main theorem three times before moving on."
cards.context: ETS uses 'reiterate' for deliberate repetition — not accidental redundancy but intentional restatement to ensure understanding or reinforce a point.
cards.mnem: RE + ITERATE → to ITERATE again (RE) — like a loop in code that runs the same statement multiple times until it lands.
cards.vis: A programmer writes one line of code: 'THE POINT IS X.' Then runs it again: 'THE POINT IS X.' Again. Again. A loop counter in the corner: ITERATION 1, 2, 3, 4... The code RE-ITERATES endlessly until the point is understood. RE-ITERATE: the same line, looped deliberately until it registers.

cards.def: To become less intense, violent, or severe; to sink to a lower level.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "The fever subsided by morning, and the patient began to recover."
cards.context: ETS uses 'subside' when something previously intense gradually diminishes — storms, pain, anger, floodwaters. The decline is natural and gradual, not forced.
cards.mnem: SUBSIDE → SUB + SIDE → SUB (below) + SIDE → the thing sinks to its SUB-level — goes below the surface, no longer visible.
cards.vis: A violent storm rages over a town. Gradually — the wind drops first. Then rain. Then thunder. Then silence. The storm has gone SUB: sunk below the surface, returning to wherever storms sleep. Its SIDE has gone underground. SUB-SIDE: intensity descending below the threshold of perception. The surface is calm again.

cards.def: To include or absorb something within a larger category or whole.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "The smaller department was subsumed into the larger division during the restructuring."
cards.context: ETS uses 'subsume' when one entity is absorbed into a larger one — it still exists but as part of the whole, losing independent identity. Common in classification, philosophy, and organizational passages.
cards.mnem: SUB + SUME → SUB (under) + SUME (consume) → to CONSUME something UNDER the larger whole — swallowed into the bigger category.
cards.vis: A small department (labeled 'SMALL TEAM') is placed on a giant CONSUMING machine's conveyor belt. It enters the machine whole. Inside: it's still there — but now it's labeled 'PART OF LARGE DIVISION.' It was SUB-SUMED: consumed into a larger body, existing under it, invisible as a separate thing.

cards.def: To overcome a difficulty or obstacle; to climb to the top of something.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "She surmounted enormous personal tragedy to become a leading scientist."
cards.context: ETS uses 'surmount' for overcoming challenges that require sustained effort — not a quick fix but a genuine conquest. The climbing metaphor is always structurally present.
cards.mnem: SUR + MOUNT → to go ABOVE the MOUNTAIN — you don't go around it; you go OVER the top.
cards.vis: A mountain labeled 'OBSTACLE' stands blocking the path. Everyone goes around. One climber puts her axe in and climbs — straight up, SUR (above) + MOUNT (mountain). She reaches the summit. Stands on TOP of the obstacle itself. SUR-MOUNT: not avoiding it — rising ABOVE it, standing on its crown. The mountain is now beneath her.

cards.def: Diverging from the main subject; only slightly or peripherally relevant.
cards.phrase: 💡 Daily life: "His tangential remarks derailed the meeting from its core agenda."
cards.context: ETS uses 'tangential' for ideas or arguments that touch the main point only briefly before heading off in a different direction — related but not central. From geometry: a tangent touches a circle at one point and then leaves.
cards.mnem: TANGENTIAL → TANGENT → a TANGENT line touches a circle at exactly ONE POINT then races off in its own direction — barely relevant, then gone.
cards.vis: A discussion (drawn as a circle) is underway. A speaker's line of thought is a TANGENT: it TOUCHES the circle at one tiny point — 'yes, that's vaguely related' — then races off at an angle in a completely different direction, never returning. TANGENTIAL: one touch, then departure. The arrow never comes back.
